context: Current closing price of AutoZone (AZO): $3536.42 USD as of 2026-04-15.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modestly bullish baseline for the S&P 500 across all three horizons, with the index currently trading around 3,500–3,550. For the one-day estimate, I anchored close to the prevailing price level, expecting mean-reversion tendencies and no major scheduled catalysts to produce dramatic moves in either direction. For the one-week horizon, I applied a slight upward drift reflecting the long-run positive expected return of equities, while acknowledging that short-term momentum and sentiment indicators were roughly neutral at the time. The one-month estimate continues that gradual drift higher, consistent with a historical monthly expected return in the range of 0.5–0.8%, though I kept the upward adjustment modest given the elevated macro uncertainty environment I was operating in.

Confidence interval widths were calibrated to reflect increasing uncertainty as the time horizon extends, which is a fundamental property of financial forecasting. At the one-day horizon, the roughly 230-point wide interval captures typical intraday-to-close and overnight volatility at a 90% confidence level, consistent with annualized volatility in the 18–22% range. By one week, the interval widens to about 480 points, incorporating the compounding of daily uncertainties and the possibility of scheduled macro events such as Fed communications, employment data, or inflation prints. At one month, the roughly 880-point interval reflects the full range of plausible macro and earnings-driven scenarios over a four-week window.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside my stated intervals include a sharper-than-expected Federal Reserve policy pivot, either more hawkish or more dovish than markets currently price, which historically has moved the index by several percent in a single session. A significant credit event, such as stress in regional banking or sovereign debt markets, could produce cascading selling that breaks below the lower bound of even the one-month interval. On the upside, an unexpected positive inflation surprise combined with strong earnings guidance could trigger a relief rally that exceeds the upper end of my ranges, particularly at the one-month horizon where multiple such catalysts could compound.

It is also worth noting that my intervals may be too narrow during periods of regime change, such as the transition from a hiking cycle to a cutting cycle or the emergence of a genuine recession signal. Liquidity conditions matter as well — thinner markets around holidays or major geopolitical events can amplify moves that would otherwise be contained within normal volatility expectations. These tail risks are real but difficult to assign precise probabilities to, which is precisely why treating these intervals as probabilistic guides rather than hard boundaries is important.
